chunks.text: Form No: HCJD/C-121 ORDER SHEET IN THE LAHORE HIGH COURT, LAHORE (JUDICIAL DEPARTMENT) Case No. Writ Petition No.29571/2013 The State through Prosecutor General, Punjab Versus Judge, Anti-Terrorism Court No.1, Lahore Sr.No.of order/ Proceedings Date of order/ Proceedings Order with signatures of Judge, and that of parties or counsel, where necessary. 16.06.2022 Mr. Humayoun Aslam, Deputy Prosecutor General. ===== Through this petition under Article 199 of the Constitution of Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973 the petitioner calls in question the validity of impugned order of learned Judge, Anti Terrorism Court No.I, Lahore whereby Public Prosecutor’s application under Section 10 ( e)(iii) & (f) of the Punjab Criminal Prosecution Service Act, 2006 read with Section 494 Cr.P.C. for withdrawal of prosecution of Case FIR No103/2004 dated 11.05.2004, in respect of offences U/S 353,186,427,336,148 & 149 PPC, 16 MPO and Section 7 of Anti-Terrorism Act, 1997, registered at P.S. Lohari Gate, Lahore was turned down. 2. Facts necessary for disposal of instant petition are that Naveed Ahmad, ASI got lodged the aforementioned case with the allegation that on 11.05.2004 at about 3.35 p.m. a procession led by Kh. Saad Rafique, MNA, who was armed with 222-bore rifle alongwith 80/90 persons arrived at Chowk Jahanda and raised slogan that Shehbaz Sharif, our tiger is arriving in Pakistan today and no force can stop him and on his arrival they will throw away the government. He used filthy language against the government and instigated the public at large in his speech. Complainant Writ Petition No.29571-13 2 alongwith others forbade Kh.

He used filthy language against the government and instigated the public at large in his speech. Complainant Writ Petition No.29571-13 2 alongwith others forbade Kh. Saad Rafique, upon which he opened straight firing upon them. Thereafter, the members of procession grappled with police party, threw stone upon them and set motor-cycle No.4582/LRA on fire. 3. Report U/S 173 Cr.P.C. was submitted. Learned Public Prosecutor filed an application under Section 10 ( e)(iii) & (f) of the Punjab Criminal Prosecution Service Act, 2006 read with Section 494 Cr.P.C. for withdrawal of prosecution of the aforementioned case, inter-alia, on the grounds that 9/10 co-accused have already been acquitted of the charge vide judgment dated 14.10.2006, whereas, accused Kh. Saad Rafique has been declared as proclaimed offender, as such the Government of the Punjab vide order dated 06.04.2013 has decided to withdraw the prosecution of said case, therefore, necessary permission in this regard may be granted. The learned Trial Court vide impugned order dated 04.07.2013 dismissed the said application, which is subject matter of this writ petition. 4. We have heard the learned Deputy Prosecutor General and gone through the record. 5. The Public Prosecutor has filed the application for withdrawal of prosecution in the aforementioned case under Section 10 (e)(iii) & (f) of the Punjab Criminal Prosecution Service Act, 2006 read with Section 494 Cr.P.C. Before proceeding further it would expedient to have a quick glance over the said provisions of law. Section 10 ( e)(iii) & (f) of the Punjab Criminal Prosecution Service Act, 2006 reads as under:- “Section-10. Powers of Prosecutor:. Writ Petition No.29571-13 3.

Powers of Prosecutor:. Writ Petition No.29571-13 3. A Prosecutor may (e ) with the consent of the Court, withdraw from the prosecution of any person either generally or in respect of any one or more of the offences for which he is being tried, after obtaining prior approval of— (i) the District Public Prosecutor, where the offence is punishable up to three years; (ii) the Prosecutor General, where the offence is punishable upto seven years; and (iii) the Government, in all other offences and for the offences triable by the Special Courts; and (f) at any stage of trial before any trial court subordinate to the High Court before the judgment is passed, the Prosecutor General or any Prosecutor specifically authorized by him, may, for reasons to be recorded in writing, inform the court on behalf of the Government that the Prosecutor shall not prosecute the accused upon the charge and thereupon all proceedings against the accused shall be stayed and he shall be discharged of and from the same: Provided that such discharge shall not amount to an acquittal unless the court directs otherwise.” 6. Section 494 Cr.P.C. reads as under:- “ Effect of withdrawal from prosecution.— Any public prosecutor may with the consent of the Court, in cases tried by jury before the return of the verdict and in other cases before the Judgment is pronounced, withdraw from the prosecution of any person either generally or in respect of any one or more of the offences for which he is tried, and upon such withdrawal,-- Writ Petition No.29571-13 4 (a) if it is made before a charge is framed, the accused shall be discharged in respect of such offence or offences; (b) if it is made after a charge has been framed, or when under this Code no charge is required, he shall be acquitted in respect of such offence or offences.” 7.

A Prosecutor may (e ) with the consent of the Court, withdraw from the prosecution of any person either generally or in respect of any one or more of the offences for which he is being tried, after obtaining prior approval of— (i) the District Public Prosecutor, where the offence is punishable up to three years; (ii) the Prosecutor General, where the offence is punishable upto seven years; and (iii) the Government, in all other offences and for the offences triable by the Special Courts; and (f) at any stage of trial before any trial court subordinate to the High Court before the judgment is passed, the Prosecutor General or any Prosecutor specifically authorized by him, may, for reasons to be recorded in writing, inform the court on behalf of the Government that the Prosecutor shall not prosecute the accused upon the charge and thereupon all proceedings against the accused shall be stayed and he shall be discharged of and from the same: Provided that such discharge shall not amount to an acquittal unless the court directs otherwise.” 6. Section 494 Cr.P.C. reads as under:- “ Effect of withdrawal from prosecution.— Any public prosecutor may with the consent of the Court, in cases tried by jury before the return of the verdict and in other cases before the Judgment is pronounced, withdraw from the prosecution of any person either generally or in respect of any one or more of the offences for which he is tried, and upon such withdrawal,-- Writ Petition No.29571-13 4 (a) if it is made before a charge is framed, the accused shall be discharged in respect of such offence or offences; (b) if it is made after a charge has been framed, or when under this Code no charge is required, he shall be acquitted in respect of such offence or offences.” 7. From the bare reading of the aforementioned provisions of law, it is manifestly clear that, a public prosecutor is vested with an authority to withdraw prosecution of any person either generally or in respect of any one or more offences but the same is subject to the “consent” of the Court. The consent of the Court implies its judicial discretion, which undoubtedly can be exercised by applying its judicial mind. While exercising its discretion, it is the bounden duty of the court to ensure that normal course of justice is not deflected for unseen reasons and there should be no indication of throttling the prosecution. In case reported as “Mir Hassan..Vs.. Tariq Saeed and 2 others (PLD 1977 SC 451)”, the Hon’ble Apex Court has enunciated the principles on the basis of which the discretion of giving or withholding “consent” in the following manner:- “The application for withdrawal can be made on many possible grounds which may include the inexpediency of prosecution on ground of public policy or in the interest of public peace, or the undesirability of permitting the prosecution to continue where there is insufficient or meagre evidence to justify a conviction. In making such an application the public prosecutor may legitimately be instructed by the Government which, under the legal system obtaining in Pakistan, is responsible for the prosecution of all cognizable offences. The discretionary power having been vested in the Writ Petition No.29571-13 5 public prosecutor by the statute, the Court acts, so to say, in a supervisory capacity, to see that the power is not abused in any manner exercised arbitrarily and contrary to the public interest so as to amount to an interference with the ordinary course of justice.

In making such an application the public prosecutor may legitimately be instructed by the Government which, under the legal system obtaining in Pakistan, is responsible for the prosecution of all cognizable offences. The discretionary power having been vested in the Writ Petition No.29571-13 5 public prosecutor by the statute, the Court acts, so to say, in a supervisory capacity, to see that the power is not abused in any manner exercised arbitrarily and contrary to the public interest so as to amount to an interference with the ordinary course of justice. The Court must, therefore, satisfy that there do exist on the record grounds to sustain the reasons advanced by the public prosecutor for his withdrawal from the prosecution. It is clear that this supervisory function of the Court can be exercised only on a consideration of all the facts and circumstances of the case available to the Court, and not in disregard of any material factor or circumstances having a bearing on the issue. At the same time, it is also clear that in undertaking this exercise the Court cannot embark upon the kind of detailed analysis of the evidence which can appropriately be undertaken only at the conclusion of a judicial trial. Any such attempt would, in our opinion, amount to throttling the prosecution or interfering with the ordinary course of justice.” 8. Similarly, in case reported as “Saad Shibli..Vs.. The State and another (PLD 1981 SC 617)” it has been observed as under:- “ A bare reading of this section discloses that the statute conferring the powers of withdrawal on the Public Prosecutor prescribes no guidelines and indicates no controlling features, except that such a power can be exercised before the judgment is pronounced and is subject to “consent of the Court”. From such a general dispensation certain consequences necessarily follow. In the first place, the power conferred is of the widest amptitude but no so wide as to amount to a fiat or ipsi dixit of the Public Prosecutor. Such a limitation necessarily follows the requirement of “consent of the Court.” It has been held that “where Court’s permission is sought or required, such a motion seeks the active exercise of the sound judicial discretion of the

In the first place, the power conferred is of the widest amptitude but no so wide as to amount to a fiat or ipsi dixit of the Public Prosecutor. Such a limitation necessarily follows the requirement of “consent of the Court.” It has been held that “where Court’s permission is sought or required, such a motion seeks the active exercise of the sound judicial discretion of the Writ Petition No.29571-13 6 Court”(22 A C J S 7). Judicial discretion of the Court is required to be exercised according to reasonably well settled principles, which are capable of being formulated and applied as standards by higher Courts when entertaining appeals against the manner in which they have been exercised. In this sense, therefore, “judicial” refers to the exercise of discretion in accordance with “objective” standards as opposed to subjective considerations of policy and expediency.” (Pages 72-73 S. A. de Smith Judicial Review of Administrative Action, Second Edition). In the case of orders passed under section 494, Cr.P.C. they are not appealable but revisable as held in Mir Hassan’s case. Next, such an exercise of judicial discretion presupposes the existence of grounds, their disclosure by the Public Prosecutor or their ascertainment by the Court and their nexus with the power sought to be exercised. The disclosure of the grounds need not to be by the Public Prosecutor alone. They may as well appear ex facie from the record of the Court, or, may otherwise comprise facts and events of which judicial notice can be taken under section 57 of the Evidence Act.” 9. On the touchstone of above guiding principles, when we go through the application of Public Prosecutor for withdrawal of prosecution of the aforementioned case, we are of the view that objective criteria relatable to public policy or public peace and administration of justice is squarely missing in the instant case. The ground that 9/10 co-accused have already been acquitted by the trial court is directly related to the detailed appreciation of the evidence, which cannot be undertaken for the purpose of deciding whether “consent” to the withdrawal of application should be accorded or not.

On the touchstone of above guiding principles, when we go through the application of Public Prosecutor for withdrawal of prosecution of the aforementioned case, we are of the view that objective criteria relatable to public policy or public peace and administration of justice is squarely missing in the instant case. The ground that 9/10 co-accused have already been acquitted by the trial court is directly related to the detailed appreciation of the evidence, which cannot be undertaken for the purpose of deciding whether “consent” to the withdrawal of application should be accorded or not. The ground agitated for withdrawal of prosecution amounts to throttling Writ Petition No.29571-13 7 the prosecution which is not permissible under the law. 10. It is important to note that the accused whose prosecution was desirous to be withdrawn by the Public Prosecutor was a politician and request for withdrawal of his prosecution was made during interim set-up of Provincial Government of the Punjab. In such like cases, the Court should be more careful and shall satisfy its judicial conscious that the Executive function of the Public Prosecutor was not improperly exercised in order to benefit a particular accused. No doubt, the legislature has empowered the Public Prosecutor to withdraw prosecution of any person either generally or in respect of any one or more offences prior to the pronouncement of judgment but at the same time a clog has been placed that the same shall be subject to the “consent of the Court”. In this way a sacred duty has been bestowed upon the court to see that the permission is not sought for on the grounds extraneous to the interest of justice and the offences which are against the State go unpunished merely for the reasons that the Government has decided not to prosecute such offenders under the law. If the accused is of the view that his case is at par to that 9/10 acquitted co-accused then the same could better be adjudged during judicial process of trial and not in isolation and secrecy of government department. We are of the view that by way of seeking withdrawal from the Prosecution, the Public Prosecutor and the Government were clearly pre-empting the Writ Petition No.29571-13 8 functioning of the Court itself, which is not warranted under the law. 11.

If the accused is of the view that his case is at par to that 9/10 acquitted co-accused then the same could better be adjudged during judicial process of trial and not in isolation and secrecy of government department. We are of the view that by way of seeking withdrawal from the Prosecution, the Public Prosecutor and the Government were clearly pre-empting the Writ Petition No.29571-13 8 functioning of the Court itself, which is not warranted under the law. 11. For what has been discussed above, we are of the considered view that the discretion exercised by the Trial Court for declining withdrawal request of the Public Prosecutor was well within its domain and while doing so it has not committed any illegality or irregularity warranting interference by this Court in its Constitutional jurisdiction. Resultantly, instant petition being devoid of any force stands dismissed. (Ali Baqar Najafi) (Asjad Javaid Ghural) Judge Judge Approved for reporting Judge Judge Azam*
